## Summary
The binding problem asks how the brain combines information processed in distributed neural populations into unified conscious percepts. Seeing a red ball involves separate processing of color (V4) and shape (IT cortex), yet we experience a unified red-ball percept. How does the brain bind these features together?

## Evidence
Proposed mechanisms include temporal synchrony (gamma-band oscillations binding distributed representations), convergence zones (anatomical convergence in higher cortical areas), and predictive coding (binding through top-down predictions). Gamma synchrony has empirical support but faces challenges: synchrony is neither necessary nor sufficient for binding, and the proposal may create a regress (what reads the synchrony?).

## Significance
The binding problem is a concrete, experimentally tractable version of the unity of consciousness question. Solving it would bridge neuroscience and philosophy by explaining how distributed processing generates unified experience, with implications for theories of consciousness and disorders like synesthesia.
